findings.summary: Settings is a single monolithic class that holds configuration for both OCR and classifier domains. Both domain packages depend on fields they do not use (e.g., classifier reads CLASSIFY_* fields while ocr reads OCR_DPI, OCR_MAX_SIDE, etc.), creating implicit coupling through a shared hub.
evidence: Settings class has 30+ fields mixing OCR-specific (OCR_DPI, OCR_MAX_SIDE, PAGE_WORKERS) and classifier-specific (CLASSIFY_PERSON_FIELD_ID, CLASSIFY_MAX_CHARS, CLASSIFY_TAXONOMY_LIMIT) concerns in a single class
Both daemon entry points import the same Settings and pass it through their entire call chain
findings.suggestion: Consider splitting Settings into a base section with shared fields and domain-specific sub-configs (OcrSettings, ClassifierSettings), or use Protocol types so each domain declares only the fields it needs.

findings.summary: common/tags.py pipeline_tag_ids() and clean_pipeline_tags() have knowledge of both OCR and classifier pipeline tag fields, coupling the shared layer to both domain-specific tag semantics.
evidence: pipeline_tag_ids() references PRE_TAG_ID, POST_TAG_ID, CLASSIFY_PRE_TAG_ID, OCR_PROCESSING_TAG_ID, CLASSIFY_PROCESSING_TAG_ID, CLASSIFY_POST_TAG_ID, and ERROR_TAG_ID -- enumerating all domain-specific tags in the shared layer
findings.suggestion: Have each domain register its own pipeline tag IDs rather than hard-coding them in the common layer, or accept tag IDs as parameters instead of reading them from Settings.

findings.summary: Pillow (PIL) is declared as a top-level project dependency but is only used by the OCR package (image_converter.py). The classifier daemon pulls in Pillow unnecessarily.
evidence: pyproject.toml lists 'Pillow~=10.4' in project.dependencies
Only src/ocr/image_converter.py imports from PIL; no classifier or common module uses it
findings.suggestion: Move Pillow to an optional dependency group (e.g., [project.optional-dependencies] ocr = ['Pillow~=10.4']) so classifier-only deployments do not pull in the imaging library.

findings.summary: pdf2image is declared as a top-level dependency but is only used by the OCR pipeline for PDF-to-image conversion.
evidence: pyproject.toml lists 'pdf2image~=1.17' in project.dependencies
Only src/ocr/image_converter.py imports pdf2image; classifier modules do not use it
findings.suggestion: Group pdf2image with Pillow under an OCR optional dependency set.

findings.summary: common/__init__.py does not define __all__ or re-export any public surface, unlike ocr/ and classifier/ which both define explicit __all__ lists. This is an inconsistency across sibling packages.
evidence: common/__init__.py contains only a docstring: '"""Shared building blocks for the OCR and classification daemons."""'
ocr/__init__.py and classifier/__init__.py both re-export key symbols and define __all__
findings.suggestion: This is a minor inconsistency. Since common is used via direct submodule imports (from common.config import Settings) rather than package-level imports, the current approach is functional. Consider adding __all__ = [] to make the empty surface explicit.

findings.summary: The Settings class in common/config.py bundles OCR-specific, classification-specific, and shared daemon settings into a single 30+ attribute class. Both daemons instantiate the full Settings even though each only uses a subset. This means every new classification setting (e.g. CLASSIFY_TAIL_PAGES) becomes visible to the OCR daemon and vice versa, blurring domain ownership.
evidence: Settings.__init__ calls _load_classification_settings() unconditionally, giving the OCR daemon attributes like CLASSIFY_MAX_PAGES, CLASSIFY_PERSON_FIELD_ID, etc. that it never uses
The OCR daemon reads only PRE_TAG_ID, POST_TAG_ID, OCR_PROCESSING_TAG_ID, OCR_DPI, OCR_MAX_SIDE, PAGE_WORKERS, DOCUMENT_WORKERS from settings, but carries all 30+ attributes
findings.suggestion: Consider splitting Settings into a shared base plus domain-specific subclasses (OcrSettings, ClassifySettings), or use composition with a shared CommonSettings and per-daemon config dataclasses.

findings.summary: common/utils.py contains exactly two functions (contains_redacted_marker and is_error_content) that are narrowly scoped to OCR error detection. The module name 'utils' does not communicate its actual responsibility and invites unrelated helpers to accumulate over time.
evidence: utils.py has 25 lines total: one regex pattern, contains_redacted_marker(), and is_error_content(). Both are only about redaction/refusal detection.
Imported by ocr/worker.py, ocr/provider.py, and classifier/quality_gates.py -- all for error/refusal content detection
findings.suggestion: Rename to common/error_detection.py or common/content_checks.py to make the module's purpose self-evident and discourage unrelated dumping.

findings.summary: PaperlessClient exposes two document-update methods (update_document and update_document_metadata) with overlapping but different signatures. update_document takes (doc_id, content, new_tags) as positional args, while update_document_metadata takes doc_id plus **kwargs. Callers must remember which to use based on whether content is included, creating an asymmetric seam.
evidence: paperless.py line 149: update_document(doc_id, content, new_tags) builds payload = {'content': content, 'tags': tags_list} and PATCHes
paperless.py line 174: update_document_metadata(doc_id, **fields) uses _METADATA_FIELDS mapping and PATCHes the same endpoint
tags.py finalize_document_with_error conditionally calls either update_document or update_document_metadata depending on whether content is provided
findings.suggestion: Unify into a single method (e.g. update_document_metadata accepting an optional content parameter) so that callers do not need to choose between two similar methods that hit the same API endpoint.

findings.summary: Tag collections are passed as set[int] in some handoffs and list[int] in others, with conversions happening at multiple boundaries rather than one canonical place. This creates minor friction at every integration seam involving tags.
evidence: extract_tags returns set[int]; clean_pipeline_tags returns set[int]; but update_document_metadata internally does list(value) when key=='tags' (paperless.py line 189)
classifier/worker.py line 205: update_document_metadata(self.doc_id, tags=list(updated)) explicitly converts set to list
ocr/worker.py line 201: update_document(self.doc_id, full_text, current_tags) passes current_tags as set[int], which update_document then converts via list(new_tags)
stale_lock.py line 45: update_document_metadata(doc_id, tags=list(updated)) -- another explicit conversion
findings.suggestion: Have PaperlessClient accept Iterable[int] for tags and handle the conversion internally in one place, removing the need for callers to convert.

findings.summary: The daemon polling loop catches a broad, manually-curated tuple of exception types (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, RuntimeError, ValueError, TypeError, KeyError). This acts as an implicit error boundary that may silently swallow unexpected errors, and must be manually kept in sync as the codebase evolves.
evidence: daemon_loop.py line 106: except (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, RuntimeError, ValueError, TypeError, KeyError) as exc -- this enumeration lacks a principled boundary; TypeError and KeyError could mask programming bugs rather than transient failures
findings.suggestion: Define a narrower set of truly-transient exceptions (OSError, httpx.HTTPError) and let programming errors (TypeError, KeyError, ValueError) propagate or be handled by a separate mechanism that logs more aggressively.

findings.summary: TaxonomyCache._get_or_create_item_id calls self.refresh() while already holding self._lock (an RLock). The refresh method also acquires self._lock, so this works only because RLock is reentrant. If the lock were ever changed to a regular Lock (e.g. during refactoring), this would deadlock. The use of RLock masks a design issue where the I/O-heavy refresh call happens inside the critical section.
evidence: taxonomy.py line 186: with self._lock: ... line 199: self.refresh() -- refresh at line 138 also acquires self._lock
self._lock is initialized as threading.RLock() at line 125, making the nested acquisition work but holding the lock across network I/O
findings.suggestion: Restructure so that refresh is called outside the lock, or extract an internal _refresh_unlocked method that _get_or_create_item_id calls while already holding the lock, making the reentrancy intentional and documented.

findings.summary: ClassificationProvider._create_with_compat uses a while True loop with an internal compat_retries counter to handle parameter-stripping retries. The loop termination depends on multiple code paths (return, continue, or reaching max retries), making it harder to reason about than a bounded for loop.
evidence: provider.py lines 74-91: while True loop with compat_retries counter incremented inside, bounded by len(_COMPAT_PARAMS) check at line 79, but also exits via return None at lines 87/91
findings.suggestion: Replace with a for loop bounded by len(_COMPAT_PARAMS)+1 iterations, making the maximum iteration count explicit in the loop construct rather than spread across conditionals.

findings.summary: Settings uses three different validation patterns for integer env vars: some use max(0, int(...)) silently clamping (e.g. LLM_MAX_CONCURRENT), some raise ValueError on invalid ranges (e.g. MAX_RETRIES), and some use _get_optional_positive_int_env which silently returns None for non-positive values. This inconsistency makes it hard to predict what happens with bad input.
evidence: config.py line 133: LLM_MAX_CONCURRENT = max(0, int(...)) -- silent clamp to 0
config.py lines 125-126: MAX_RETRIES raises ValueError if < 1
config.py line 186-188: _get_optional_positive_int_env returns None for value <= 0 -- silent conversion of positive intent to disabled
config.py line 138: PAGE_WORKERS = max(1, int(...)) -- silent clamp to 1
findings.suggestion: Adopt a consistent policy: either all settings clamp silently (documenting ranges), or all settings validate and raise. A small _get_clamped_int_env helper could standardize this.

findings.summary: OcrProcessor.process() has a deeply nested control flow: a top-level try/finally wrapping a nested try/finally for image cleanup, containing multiple early returns. The finally block references variables (claimed, success, start_time) set at different points, requiring careful reading to track which paths set which state.
evidence: ocr/worker.py lines 61-135: process() has claimed=False and success=False initialized before try, a nested try/finally for image cleanup at lines 97-104, and the outer finally at line 120 that references both claimed and start_time

The success flag is only set at line 119 after _update_paperless_document, but _update_paperless_document can itself route to _finalize_with_error on line 187-192, meaning success=True does not always mean the document was successfully processed
findings.suggestion: Extract the inner image-processing block (download, convert, OCR, assemble) into a separate method that returns its result or raises, simplifying the main process() to a linear sequence of steps with a single try/finally.

findings.summary: The Settings class in config.py aggregates 30+ fields spanning OCR, classification, daemon, logging, and image processing concerns into a single flat class. Every consumer receives the full Settings object even when it only needs a narrow slice (e.g., OcrProvider only needs AI_MODELS, OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS, OCR_MAX_SIDE, REQUEST_TIMEOUT, and retry params).
evidence: Settings.__init__ calls 7 separate _load_* methods covering unrelated domains
OcrProvider receives full Settings but only touches ~6 fields
ClassificationProvider receives full Settings but only touches ~4 fields plus CLASSIFY_* fields
daemon_loop.run_polling_threadpool receives individual params rather than Settings, showing the tension
findings.suggestion: Consider grouping related settings into nested typed objects (e.g., LLMSettings, TagSettings, ImageSettings) or at minimum defining Protocol types for the narrow slices each consumer needs, which would make dependencies explicit.

findings.summary: ClassificationProcessor.process() orchestrates 9 distinct steps in a single method body spanning ~70 lines with multiple early returns, nested conditionals, and a try/finally block. While each step is individually clear, the method mixes concern detection (error tag check, empty content, refusal markers, generic type) with orchestration.
evidence: process() lines 66-152: checks error tag, claims lock, checks empty content, checks refusal markers, truncates, calls LLM, validates empty result, validates generic type, applies classification -- all in one method
Four separate early-return paths each calling _finalize_with_error with slightly different context
findings.suggestion: Extract the validation checks (error tag, empty content, refusal markers, generic type) into a single guard method that returns a reason-string or None, reducing process() to: refresh -> guard -> truncate -> classify -> apply.

findings.summary: OpenAIChatMixin is a mixin that implicitly couples to the concrete _OpenAIClientHolder singleton and requires subclasses to have a 'settings' attribute with specific retry fields. This implicit contract is documented only in a docstring rather than enforced by the type system.
evidence: OpenAIChatMixin._create_completion accesses _openai_holder.get() directly -- a module-level singleton
The mixin docstring states 'The mixin expects self.settings to expose MAX_RETRIES and MAX_RETRY_BACKOFF_SECONDS' but this is not enforced via Protocol or abstract base
Only two concrete implementations exist (OcrProvider, ClassificationProvider), both following identical init patterns
findings.suggestion: Since there are only two consumers with near-identical structure, a composition-based approach (passing a pre-built completion callable) would make the dependency explicit. Alternatively, add a Protocol type hint for the settings requirement.

findings.summary: The module-level functions init_openai_client(), get_openai_client(), and is_openai_client_ready() in llm.py are thin forwarders to the _openai_holder singleton, adding an indirection layer without policy or translation.
evidence: init_openai_client simply calls _openai_holder.init(client)
get_openai_client simply calls _openai_holder.get()
is_openai_client_ready simply calls _openai_holder.is_ready()
The mixin itself bypasses these functions and accesses _openai_holder.get() directly (line 84)
findings.suggestion: Either expose _openai_holder directly (renaming to openai_holder) or have the mixin use the public functions consistently. The current split where external callers use the functions but the mixin uses the holder directly is inconsistent.

findings.summary: The classifier and ocr packages define __init__.py with __all__ and re-exports, while the common package's __init__.py contains only a docstring and no re-exports. This is an inconsistent export pattern across sibling packages.
evidence: classifier/__init__.py re-exports 5 symbols with __all__
ocr/__init__.py re-exports 5 symbols with __all__
common/__init__.py contains only a docstring: 'Shared building blocks for the OCR and classification daemons.'
findings.suggestion: Either add key re-exports to common/__init__.py (e.g., Settings, PaperlessClient) for consistency, or document why common deliberately avoids re-exports (e.g., it is an internal package not meant to be imported as a unit).

findings.summary: OcrProvider.transcribe_image manually calls self._stats.inc('attempts') inline, while ClassificationProvider delegates attempt tracking to the _create_with_compat method. The two providers that share OpenAIChatMixin follow different patterns for when and how stats are incremented.
evidence: OcrProvider.transcribe_image line 97: self._stats.inc('attempts') before self._create_completion()
ClassificationProvider._create_with_compat line 76: self._stats.inc('attempts') inside a while-loop wrapper before self._create_completion()
OcrProvider does not use _create_with_compat at all -- it calls _create_completion directly
findings.suggestion: Consider extracting a shared completion-with-stats method or having both providers use _create_with_compat (which already handles the attempt counting), so the stat tracking protocol is consistent across siblings.

findings.summary: finalize_document_with_error does not wrap API calls in try/except PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS, unlike every sibling function in tags.py (release_processing_tag, remove_stale_queue_tag, get_latest_tags). If the Paperless API is unreachable during error finalization, the exception propagates up through the worker and is caught only by the generic daemon_loop catch-all, producing an opaque 'Work item failed' log instead of a targeted 'Failed to finalize with error tag' message.
evidence: tags.py:116-148 calls client.update_document and client.update_document_metadata without try/except
tags.py:65-78 (remove_stale_queue_tag) wraps same calls in try/except PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS
tags.py:91-113 (release_processing_tag) wraps same calls in try/except PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS
tags.py:38-49 (get_latest_tags) wraps same call in try/except PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS
findings.suggestion: Wrap the two API calls in finalize_document_with_error with try/except PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS and log a specific failure message, consistent with the error handling pattern used by every other function in the same module.

findings.summary: daemon_loop.py line 106 catches a manually enumerated exception tuple (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, RuntimeError, ValueError, TypeError, KeyError) instead of referencing the centralized PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS constant. This tuple is a superset that also adds RuntimeError and TypeError, creating a parallel exception policy that can drift independently from the canonical definition in paperless.py.
evidence: daemon_loop.py:106 catches (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, RuntimeError, ValueError, TypeError, KeyError)
paperless.py:22 defines PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS = (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, ValueError, KeyError)
Every other call site in the codebase uses PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS by reference
findings.suggestion: Define a named constant (e.g., DAEMON_LOOP_EXCEPTIONS) that composes PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS with the additional types, or add a comment explaining why the superset is intentional. This makes the relationship explicit and prevents silent drift.

findings.summary: Worker unit tests (test_worker.py for both OCR and classifier) patch 4-6 internal module references per test (bytes_to_images, assemble_full_text, claim_processing_tag, release_processing_tag, etc.) and assert on mock call counts and argument shapes. These tests are tightly coupled to implementation wiring rather than observable behavior, meaning any internal refactor (e.g., inlining a helper or changing call order) breaks tests even when behavior is unchanged.
evidence: test_worker.py:40-43 patches four module-level references for a single test
Tests assert mock_claim.assert_called_once(), mock_release.assert_called_once() etc. which test wiring not behavior
findings.suggestion: Consider adding a few higher-level worker tests that inject fake collaborators (rather than patching imports) and assert on observable outcomes (e.g., what metadata was sent to paperless_client) rather than internal call sequences. The existing integration tests partially address this but don't cover error paths.

findings.summary: 949 occurrences of '# Arrange', '# Act', '# Assert' comments appear across 16 test files. While the AAA pattern is sound, these comments are mechanical markers that restate the obvious structure of every test without adding insight. They are a hallmark of AI-generated test code and add visual noise.
evidence: 949 total '# Arrange/Act/Assert' comments across 16 files
test_tags.py uses the pattern on trivial 3-line tests like test_returns_set_of_ints_from_doc_tags
test_claims.py uses it on every single test method without exception
findings.suggestion: Remove the AAA comments. The test structure is already clear from the code itself. If a test is complex enough to need section markers, it may benefit from extraction into helper methods instead.

findings.summary: Several short functions have docstrings that restate the function name and signature without adding domain insight. Examples: 'Return ``True`` if the image is essentially blank (all white).' on is_blank, 'Return how many documents reference this taxonomy item.' on _get_usage_count, 'Deduplicate a model list while preserving insertion order.' on unique_models (a one-liner).
evidence: llm.py:89-91 unique_models has a docstring for a single-line `list(dict.fromkeys(models))`

taxonomy.py:60-78 _get_usage_count docstring restates 'Return how many documents reference this taxonomy item' but the Paperless version-compat logic in the body is the non-obvious part that deserves explanation
findings.suggestion: For trivial functions, either omit the docstring or use it to explain WHY (e.g., version compatibility reasons) rather than restating WHAT the code already shows.
